sound_type: rhythmic loud body percussion -- Southern Resident Killer Whale striking water surface with pectoral fins in sequence, 5-20+ slaps, rhythmic interval, E=7
ecosystem: Open water -- Salish Sea, San Juan Islands, Puget Sound
season: Resident (year-round Southern Residents); more frequent in social contexts, post-hunt, super-pod events
milestone: Orca arc degree 5 of 7 (degrees 270-276). Body percussion documented: pectoral fin as percussion instrument. Social behavior in post-hunt and super-pod contexts. E=7 following E=8 spy-hop.

coordinates.description: Southern Resident range, pec-slapping throughout range but particularly in social aggregation areas: San Juan Islands, Haro Strait, Boundary Pass.

behavioral_context.pec_slap_description: Orca rolls to expose pectoral fin. Fin raised above surface. Fin brought down against water: sharp crack. Sequence: 5-20+ slaps with rhythmic interval. Creates rhythmic acoustic pattern above and below surface.
behavioral_context.anatomy_note: Adult male pectoral fin: ~1.8 meters length, ~0.9 m width at broadest. Internally: humerus, radius, ulna, modified digits -- homologous to mammalian forelimb. The pec-slap is evolutionarily a mammal striking water with its arm.
behavioral_context.function: Acoustic communication (underwater propagation), social arousal (contagious in group contexts), post-hunt positive arousal expression, possible sexual display. Multifunctional.
behavioral_context.social_context: Super-pod events, post-hunt socializing, aggregated pods. Contagious: one individual slaps, others follow. More frequent in male orcas in some contexts.

sound_metrics.amplitude: E=7. Significant per-strike impact. Broadband pressure wave above and below surface. Audible at hundreds of meters in calm water. Total energy of full sequence is substantial. Lower per-event energy than breach (E=9) but rhythmic and sustained.
sound_metrics.frequency_range_hz: Broadband. Primary energy 50 Hz to a few kHz. Low-to-mid frequency dominant. Distinct from high-frequency click trains of echolocation (degree 276).
sound_metrics.key_property: E=7 rhythmic body percussion. Pectoral fin as percussion instrument. Sequences of 5-20+ strikes with consistent interval. Social/post-hunt context. Orca arc degree 5/7. Body as drum; water as drum skin.

nodes.title: Body as Percussion Instrument
nodes.description: Pectoral fin (homologous to mammalian forelimb: humerus, radius, ulna, modified digits) strikes water surface in rhythmic sequence. E=7. Not explosive (breach) but sustained and rhythmic. The body as drum.

nodes.title: Acoustic Communication -- Underwater Rhythmic Signal
nodes.description: Each pec-slap creates underwater pressure wave audible to other orcas at distance. Rhythmic sequence creates patterned acoustic signal. Long-range percussion: location, behavioral state, or individual signaling.

nodes.title: Social Context -- Super-Pods, Post-Hunt, Arousal
nodes.description: Super-pod events increase pec-slapping. Post-hunt positive arousal: follows successful Chinook salmon foraging. Contagious in groups. Social bonding display function.

nodes.title: Orca Arc Degree 5/7 -- Behavioral Repertoire
nodes.description: Degrees 270-276. 273 was energy maximum (breach E=9). 274 observation (spy-hop E=8). 275 percussion (pec-slap E=7). 276 closes with echolocation E=8. Energy oscillation in final arc entries.

cross_references.relationship: Preceding entry. Spy-hop: observation/intake (visual). Pec-slap: output (acoustic). Different functional modes of above-surface behavior.
Arc closes. Surface mechanical percussion (pec-slap) to internal biosonar (echolocation, 224 dB). External body to internal acoustic architecture.
Post-hunt pec-slapping follows successful Chinook foraging. Salmon at 237; orca social behavior expressing successful salmon capture at 275.
Combined E=12. Rhythmic body percussion meets rhythmic lo-fi punk. Olympia enters engine. Both find rhythm in simplicity.
